# Transcrição: 06- Underwear and Rain

- **Fonte:** 06- Underwear and Rain
- **Duração:** 07:01

## [00:00 - 03:01]

I hear her scratching. I open my eyes and I look over the bed and I can see her even in the dark. I can see her eyes, my little best friend, Kaylee. She's a white, lasa opso. And if you own a dog, you know that even though they don't speak English, they can talk to you with their eyes. And I can see even in the dark what she's trying to tell me. She has to pee. And I'm surprised because she's not a middle of the night pee kind of dog. So I look at my phone, I see it's two o'clock. I realize I have to bring her out. And now I have a decision to make. I'm wearing a pair of satin boxers covered in purple and red hearts. My mother-in-law gave them to me for Valentine's Day, a fact I try to forget every time I put them on. But I have to decide if I'm going to get dressed and bring her out or if I'm just going to run her out and get back to bed as quick as I can. It's warm and I live on one of these streets that you only drive on if you live on. There's a dozen houses. I know everybody, they're all in bed. It's two o'clock. I decide this will be fine. I slide some slippers on and we head for the door. When we get to the stairs, I pick Kaylee up. About 10 years ago, she had emergency spinal surgery that she wasn't supposed to survive. And if she did survive, she was supposed to never walk again. And she beat both of those odds. But she's never done stairs ever since. And so I always hold her close and I bring her up and down every stair she ever has to climb. And so when I get to the bottom of the stairs, I hook the leash on and we go out onto the lawn and she starts to find a spot. It's a warm night. It's dark. There's not a star or moon in the sky. And the birds are loud. They're like riotous. And I'm waiting for her to pee and finally she does. And I turn to go back in and she pulls on the leash and I look. She wants to walk to the end of the street. And she doesn't walk very much anymore. And I want to go to bed. So I'm like, come on. And she gives me another look with her eyes. It's the look that says, don't be an asshole. And so I say, fine. It's like 100 yards down the road. It'll be fine. And so in my underwear and slippers, I begin walking my dog down the road. I really can't say no to Kaylee ever anyway. When we got her, I was married to another woman. It was my practice marriage. And when my ex-wife said, let's get a dog, I said, no, because I will fall in love and you have no follow through. And so you will abandon the dog and want to get rid of it and it will become between us. And that's exactly what happened. In honesty, the marriage would have eventually fallen apart anyway, but truly Kaylee got me out of that marriage at least a year before I would have. And that meant that I got to meet the woman who I love today, the woman of my dreams. And I would have not been in a position to meet her at that time had it not been for

## [03:01 - 06:02]

my dog, my friend. And so I have a hard time saying no. And so we finally get to the end of the street and I turn to head back and she pulls again. And I look and she's already rounding the corner. She doesn't walk around the block. And I am in my underwear and I want to go to bed. So I said, come on, really? And she's already going. And this next street will be fine too. Nobody drives on this street. But the third street is Main Street in Newington, Connecticut. There will be cars and trucks and cops. Everyone will be on that street. But I see guys run around in shorts and no shirt. And I know I don't look like any of those guys, but maybe, maybe that's the goal. Maybe that's why I'm out at two o'clock in the morning. So I don't subject people to this. And so I decide to walk around the block in my underwear with my dog. We turn the right on Main Street. I'm now as far away from my house as I can be on this block. And it is that moment when one second ago it was not raining. And now the biblical blood, flood, all the rain in the world just instantly falls down upon me. And now I know why there were no stars in the sky and why the birds were making a racket. Like I get it. And I start to think maybe she did this too. Kayleigh, because she used to love to run in the rain. We both did. We would run everywhere in the rain and we haven't done it in years. And I'm mad now. Like I realize she has tricked me. And so I bend over to pick her up and I see a look on her face that I don't see very much anymore. She is smiling. She has that tongue out of her mouth smile. And I have this moment where I realize that my dog is 16 years old and she, I know in my heart, isn't doing very well. And this might be the last time in my life and in her life that we ever walk in the rain together. And so I stop and I stand upright and I stand in my underwear. It's becoming like disturbingly plastered on my parts. But I stand there for five, 10, 15 minutes. I wait until she is ready to go before we leave. Kayleigh died three months ago. And on that corner that night, it turns out I was wrong and I was right. I was wrong to think she didn't have a lot of time left because she had two more years, two glorious years that I had with my best friend. But I was right too, because it was the last time we ever walked in the rain together that night in my underwear. Except honestly, until this very second, it does not occur to me.

## [06:02 - 07:01]

But tonight right here for one second, you all went away and this went away. And that storytelling, for one second, I was back on that corner with my friend, who I miss every day of my life. And I could see her smiling at me as clear as day. And maybe you could too. I hope you could. Because she was beautiful and she was perfect. And every morning that I wake up, I look for her before realizing that she's gone. And so if you could see her, I'm glad. And I saw her tonight. And for that, I thank you. You
